pr_summary: This PR adds new functionality to the codebase.
verdict_reason: The review found critical-severity issues requiring attention.

findings.title: Infinite Loop with No Timeout
findings.description: The while loop in the pollOrderStatus function will continue indefinitely if the order status remains 'pending'. This can cause the service to hang and consume excessive resources.
findings.suggestion: Add a timeout or a maximum number of retries to the loop to prevent it from running indefinitely.

findings.title: INSECURE DIRECT OBJECT REFERENCE (IDOR) in Order Cancellation
findings.description: The cancelOrder function does not verify if the requesting user owns the order being cancelled, allowing an attacker to cancel any order. This is a high-severity issue as it allows unauthorized access to sensitive data.
findings.suggestion: Add a check to ensure the order belongs to the requesting user, e.g., `if (order.userId !== userId) { throw new Error('Unauthorized'); }`

findings.title: NULL DEREFERENCE / UNHANDLED NULLS
findings.description: The order.userEmail is not checked for null before passing it to the notification service, which can cause a null pointer exception if the order does not have a user email.
findings.suggestion: Add a null check: if (!order.userEmail) { throw new Error(`Order does not have a user email: ${orderId}`); }

findings.title: INSECURE DIRECT OBJECT REFERENCE (IDOR) in Order Status Polling
findings.description: The pollOrderStatus function does not verify if the requesting user owns the order being polled, allowing an attacker to access sensitive data. This is a high-severity issue as it allows unauthorized access to sensitive data.
findings.suggestion: Add a check to ensure the order belongs to the requesting user, e.g., `if (order.userId !== userId) { throw new Error('Unauthorized'); }`

findings.title: INSECURE DIRECT OBJECT REFERENCE (IDOR) in Bulk Order Cancellation
findings.description: The bulkCancelOrders function calls cancelOrder with a hardcoded 'system' user ID, potentially allowing an attacker to cancel any order. This is a high-severity issue as it allows unauthorized access to sensitive data.
findings.suggestion: Pass the actual requesting user ID to the cancelOrder function, e.g., `await cancelOrder(id, userId);`

findings.title: Missing test: bulkCancelOrders with empty orderIds array
findings.description: If the orderIds array is empty, the function will not throw an error but will not perform any actions either. A test should be added to verify the behavior in this case.
findings.suggestion: Test bulkCancelOrders with an empty orderIds array, verify that it does not throw an error and does not perform any actions.

findings.line_content: const discounts: any = { SAVE10: 0.1, SAVE20: 0.2, SAVE50: 0.5 };
findings.title: Unvalidated User Input in Discount Calculation
findings.description: The calculateDiscount function uses a user-controlled input (discountCode) without validation, potentially allowing an attacker to manipulate the discount amount. This is a low-severity issue as it may lead to incorrect discount calculations.
findings.suggestion: Validate the discountCode before applying the discount, e.g., `if (!Object.keys(discounts).includes(discountCode)) { throw new Error('Invalid discount code'); }`

positive_observations: The code follows a consistent structure.
Function naming is clear and descriptive.
missing_tests: If the orderIds array is empty, the function will not throw an error but will not perform any actions either. A test should be added to verify the behavior in this case.
